response_text:
This is a good question that gets at an important nuance. The short answer is **no** - and understanding why reveals something important about Rust's design.

## What the Borrow Checker Actually Does

The borrow checker enforces ownership rules **at compile time**, which eliminates a specific class of memory errors. But it has a fundamental limitation: it requires ownership relationships to be **statically determinable**.

## When You Still Need Reference Counting

**Shared ownership with unknown lifetimes:**
```rust
// This won't compile - who owns the data?
let a = SomeData::new();
let b = &a; // b borrows from a
drop(a);    // but a is gone!
use(b);     // dangling reference - caught at compile time
```

When multiple parts of your program need to **own** the same data and you can't determine at compile time which will live longest, you need `Rc<T>` or `Arc<T>`:

```rust
use std::rc::Rc;

let shared = Rc::new(SomeData::new());
let clone1 = Rc::clone(&shared);
let clone2 = Rc::clone(&shared);
// Data lives until ALL clones are dropped
```

**Common real cases:**
- Graph data structures with cycles
- Shared caches
- Observer patterns
- Some async/concurrent scenarios (Arc specifically)

## The Honest Tradeoff

| Approach | Cost | Guarantee |
|----------|------|-----------|
| Borrow checker | Zero runtime cost | Compile-time proof |
| Rc/Arc | Runtime overhead | Flexible shared ownership |

## A Specific Limitation Worth Knowing

Even `Rc`/`Arc` don't handle **cycles** - you can still leak memory:

```rust
use std::rc::Rc;
use std::cell::RefCell;

// These two nodes reference each other
// Neither ever reaches count of zero
// Memory leaks
```

For cycles you need `Weak<T>` references, which is something garbage collectors handle automatically.

## The Practical Reality

Rust gives you a **spectrum of tools** rather than one solution:
- Borrow checker for the common case (zero cost)
